questions.stem: A team ingests JSON files that arrive continuously in cloud storage and wants automatic file discovery with serverless orchestration. Which Snowflake feature is the best fit?
questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpipe auto-ingest is designed to discover new files through event notifications and load them continuously. B is manual, C handles disaster recovery and copying databases, and D accelerates selective queries rather than ingestion.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpipe auto-ingest

questions.stem: A company wants to share curated data products with external consumers without copying data into the consumer account. Which Snowflake capability best meets that requirement?

options.text: Cloning the database into the consumer account
Unloading to cloud storage and emailing URLs

questions.explanation: A is correct because Secure Data Sharing exposes live data without copying it into the consumer account. B creates another copy, C is not governed live sharing, and D addresses replication/failover rather than external sharing.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Secure Data Sharing overview

questions.stem: An organization needs to move the same raw data feed into Snowflake accounts running in multiple cloud regions. Which design is usually the most appropriate?

options.text: Load once into one account and use replication / failover or cross-account sharing patterns as needed
Require every consumer to run SELECT against the producer warehouse directly
Convert all data to stages but never load it

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake supports data replication and sharing patterns for multi-region distribution. B tightly couples consumers to one warehouse, C is session-scoped, and D does not deliver queryable data.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Replication and data sharing concepts

questions.stem: Which object stores the metadata needed to access files in cloud storage from Snowflake?
questions.explanation: A is correct because stages define where files live and how Snowflake reaches them. Masking policies protect data, streams track table changes, and warehouse monitors track credits.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — CREATE STAGE

questions.stem: A pipeline must load files repeatedly but avoid reloading the same files after successful processing. Which Snowflake loading behavior is most relevant?
questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake tracks loaded files for COPY-based loads and can avoid reprocessing them unless forced. The other choices govern query reuse, compute suspension, or statement runtime.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — COPY INTO <table> load metadata and history

questions.stem: A source system sends CDC events and the target pipeline needs sub-minute ingestion with minimal infrastructure management. Which option is usually strongest inside Snowflake?

options.text: Snowpipe Streaming or continuous ingestion patterns

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpipe Streaming / continuous ingestion targets low-latency data arrival with limited operational overhead. B is not near real time, C is unrelated to ingestion, and D improves query selectivity not ingest latency.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Continuous data ingestion / Snowpipe Streaming

questions.stem: When designing an ingestion pipeline for semi-structured data with evolving attributes, which landing-table pattern is most flexible initially?

options.text: Store the payload in VARIANT before downstream normalization
Flatten every possible field into fixed columns before loading any data
Load into a temporary table only
Reject all records with any optional field changes

questions.explanation: A is correct because VARIANT is designed for flexible semi-structured ingestion and allows schema-on-read or later transformations. B overcommits early, C is not durable, and D is unnecessarily brittle.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Querying semi-structured data and VARIANT

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature lets a team create a point-in-time copy of a table almost instantly without physically duplicating all storage blocks?
questions.explanation: A is correct because zero-copy cloning uses shared micro-partition storage until changes occur. The other features are for precomputed queries, ingestion, or result reuse.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Zero-copy cloning

questions.stem: A team wants to recover a table after an accidental delete that happened earlier today. Which Snowflake capability is specifically designed for this scenario?
questions.explanation: A is correct because Time Travel enables querying and restoring historical table states within the retention period. The other features are unrelated to point-in-time recovery.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Time Travel

questions.stem: What is the main benefit of separating raw, refined, and curated pipeline layers into distinct schemas or databases?

options.text: It improves governance, lineage, and operational clarity across pipeline stages
It guarantees queries will never need warehouses
It disables storage costs on raw data
It automatically creates streams on every table

questions.explanation: A is correct because layered organization makes ownership, quality boundaries, and promotion paths clearer. B, C, and D are not automatic outcomes of schema separation.
questions.reference: Snowflake official certification overview — end-to-end pipeline design and sharing

questions.stem: A producer account wants consumers to see only approved rows and columns from a shared dataset. Which design best supports that goal?

options.text: Share secure views instead of raw base tables
Only grant USAGE on the warehouse
Use a transient table and hope consumers filter correctly

questions.explanation: A is correct because secure views let the provider curate and govern exposed data while sharing live results. The other options do not provide the same governed presentation layer.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Secure views and Secure Data Sharing

questions.stem: A pipeline must be portable across environments with minimal code changes. Which practice helps most?

options.text: Parameterize environment-specific object names and integration settings
Hard-code all warehouse, stage, and schema names in SQL
Keep all logic only in interactive worksheets
Merge prod and dev into one schema

questions.explanation: A is correct because environment parameters reduce deployment friction and accidental coupling. Hard-coding, worksheet-only logic, and shared dev/prod schemas all reduce portability and safety.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — deployment best practices / object parameterization concepts

questions.stem: For a near-real-time pipeline that must react only when upstream data changes, which pair of features is historically used together?
questions.explanation: A is correct because streams capture changes and tasks schedule or trigger downstream processing. The other pairs solve different governance, performance, or sharing problems.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Streams and tasks overview

questions.stem: What does a standard stream primarily record?

options.text: Change data capture between the current offset and the previous consumed offset
Every query result returned from a warehouse

questions.explanation: A is correct because streams expose change tracking information for table/view changes relative to the stream offset. The other answers describe unrelated metadata.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Introduction to streams

questions.stem: Which function is commonly used in a task condition to determine whether a stream currently has unread changes?
questions.explanation: A is correct because the documented SYSTEM stream-has-data helper checks whether a stream contains unread change data. The other functions do not test stream contents.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — stream-has-data system function

questions.stem: What happens to a stream offset when a transaction successfully consumes the stream in downstream DML?

options.text: The offset advances to the transaction’s ending point
All historical change data becomes permanent query history only

questions.explanation: A is correct because the stream offset advances after successful consumption in a transaction. Streams are not dropped automatically, source tables are untouched, and query history is separate.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Stream offsets and consumption behavior

questions.stem: Which statement about triggered tasks is most accurate?

options.text: They can start based on a WHEN condition instead of only a cron schedule
They always require a fixed 1-minute cron expression
They can run only after manual RESUME each time

questions.explanation: A is correct because triggered task patterns use WHEN conditions, often with stream checks, to control execution. B, C, and D are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — CREATE TASK and task WHEN conditions

questions.stem: A root task launches several dependent child tasks. What is this structure called in Snowflake?
questions.explanation: A is correct because related parent/child tasks form a task graph. The other options refer to different Snowflake features.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Task graphs

questions.stem: A stream is created on a table, but no consumer reads it for an extended period while source data continues to change. What risk becomes important?

options.text: Change tracking data may become stale if retention is insufficient for the stream offset
The warehouse automatically drops the stream
All future tasks are suspended permanently

questions.explanation: A is correct because streams depend on retained change tracking metadata; if the offset falls outside available retention, the stream can become stale. The other outcomes are not normal stream behavior.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Stream staleness and retention considerations

questions.stem: Which command directly shows whether a task is STARTED or SUSPENDED?
questions.explanation: A is correct because SHOW TASKS includes task state information. SHOW STREAMS lists streams, DESCRIBE WAREHOUSE describes compute, and CURRENT_ROLE returns the active role.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — SHOW TASKS

questions.stem: Why might a developer choose an append-only stream?

options.text: When only inserted rows matter and delete/update tracking is unnecessary
When they need full historical Time Travel replacement
When they want to mask columns automatically
When they need to accelerate geospatial joins

questions.explanation: A is correct because append-only streams are optimized for insert-focused use cases. They do not replace Time Travel, governance policies, or performance features for specialized queries.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Append-only streams

questions.stem: A task runs a MERGE from a stream into a target table. Why is wrapping the read and write in one transaction important?

options.text: It aligns data application with stream offset advancement for consistency
It forces the task to use serverless compute only
It converts the target into a transient table

questions.explanation: A is correct because transactional consumption prevents the offset from advancing without the downstream changes committing successfully. B, C, and D are unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Streams consumption in transactions

questions.stem: What is the main difference between TASK_HISTORY and SHOW TASKS?

options.text: TASK_HISTORY shows execution history, while SHOW TASKS shows object metadata and current state
TASK_HISTORY shows streams, while SHOW TASKS shows warehouses
SHOW TASKS can only be used by ACCOUNTADMIN

questions.explanation: A is correct because TASK_HISTORY is historical execution telemetry and SHOW TASKS is object state/definition metadata. The other statements are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — TASK_HISTORY and SHOW TASKS

questions.stem: Which behavior is most accurate for a stream on a view?

options.text: It tracks change results exposed by the view definition, subject to documented limitations
It is identical to a materialized view refresh log in every respect
It can only be created on temporary views

questions.explanation: A is correct because streams on views track changes represented through the view, but Snowflake documents limitations and requirements. B, C, and D are incorrect overstatements.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Streams on views

questions.stem: A chain of tasks should stop when an upstream task fails. Which design supports that most naturally?

options.text: Model the workflow as dependent tasks in a task graph
Run all SQL manually in separate worksheets
Use a masking policy on the task owner role

questions.explanation: A is correct because task graphs encode dependencies so downstream tasks run according to upstream completion behavior. The other choices do not orchestrate dependencies.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Task graphs and dependencies

questions.stem: What does SUSPEND TASK primarily do?

options.text: Prevents future scheduled or triggered runs until the task is resumed
Forces the source stream offset to advance

questions.explanation: A is correct because suspending a task disables future execution without deleting the object. The definition, history, and stream offsets remain separate concerns.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — ALTER TASK ... SUSPEND | RESUME

questions.stem: A task should execute SQL without binding to a dedicated warehouse. Which compute option is specifically designed for that?
questions.explanation: A is correct because serverless tasks use Snowflake-managed compute for task execution. The other options are not task compute modes.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Serverless tasks

questions.stem: Why might a task WHEN clause be preferable to running the task every minute unconditionally?

options.text: It can avoid unnecessary executions when no new data is available
It increases retention on every table automatically
It removes the need for streams
It guarantees lower storage cost on all objects

questions.explanation: A is correct because WHEN conditions allow event- or data-aware execution, often reducing waste. They do not automatically change retention, remove streams, or guarantee all storage savings.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Task WHEN conditions

questions.stem: A developer updates and deletes rows in a source table. Which stream type is usually needed if downstream logic must react to all those net row changes?
questions.explanation: A is correct because standard streams track inserted and deleted row states needed for full CDC-style logic. Append-only streams omit some change categories, and the other options are unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Standard streams

questions.stem: What is a dynamic table designed to do?

options.text: Materialize query results and refresh them automatically to meet a target lag

questions.explanation: A is correct because dynamic tables maintain query results automatically according to a freshness objective. The other answers describe unrelated features.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables overview

questions.stem: What does TARGET_LAG control on a dynamic table?

options.text: How stale the materialized result is allowed to be relative to upstream changes
How long query results remain in cache
How many rows are kept in Time Travel
How many clusters a warehouse can use

questions.explanation: A is correct because TARGET_LAG expresses a freshness objective for dynamic table maintenance. The other options refer to result cache, retention, and warehouse scaling.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — CREATE DYNAMIC TABLE / TARGET_LAG

questions.stem: Compared with a streams-and-tasks pipeline, what is a major advantage of dynamic tables for many transformation pipelines?

options.text: They reduce orchestration complexity by letting Snowflake manage refresh dependencies
They can only operate on static CSV files
They automatically expose data to external accounts

questions.explanation: A is correct because dynamic tables can simplify orchestration and dependency management. They still incur compute, support broader sources than static CSV, and do not replace sharing features.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables compared to streams and tasks

questions.stem: When would streams and tasks still be preferable to dynamic tables?

options.text: When the pipeline needs explicit CDC-style procedural control over row-by-row change application
When no data changes ever occur
When SQL should never be used

questions.explanation: A is correct because streams/tasks remain useful for explicit change consumption and imperative orchestration. B, C, and D do not meaningfully distinguish the features.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables compared to streams and tasks

questions.stem: A team defines multiple dynamic tables in a transformation chain. What does Snowflake manage automatically within that graph?

options.text: Refresh dependency ordering to satisfy freshness goals

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake manages refresh ordering in dynamic table pipelines. The other options are unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic table refresh behavior

questions.stem: Which statement is most accurate about using a dynamic table as a source for further processing?

options.text: It can participate in downstream transformations and can also be compared with streams/tasks patterns
It can only feed dashboards, not SQL
It replaces all base tables in the account

questions.explanation: A is correct because dynamic tables are queryable objects that can participate in layered pipelines. B, C, and D are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables usage patterns

questions.stem: If a dynamic table is not meeting freshness expectations, what setting is most likely to be revisited first?
questions.explanation: A is correct because TARGET_LAG directly describes the freshness objective. The other session parameters do not govern dynamic table refresh recency.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic table target lag

questions.stem: A developer is deciding between a materialized view and a dynamic table for a multi-step transformation. Which feature is generally more flexible for pipeline-style transformations?
questions.explanation: A is correct because dynamic tables are aimed at transformation pipelines with managed refresh logic, whereas materialized views are narrower query acceleration structures. The other options are unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables compared to materialized views / streams and tasks

questions.stem: What is one practical reason to keep raw ingestion tables separate from downstream dynamic tables?

options.text: It preserves a stable landing zone while transformations refresh independently

questions.explanation: A is correct because raw landing tables and downstream dynamic tables serve different responsibilities and lifecycles. B, C, and D are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic table design patterns

questions.stem: Which tradeoff is most important when choosing a smaller TARGET_LAG?

options.text: Fresher data usually requires more refresh activity and potentially more compute
Smaller target lag guarantees zero storage cost
Smaller target lag disables query access during refresh
Smaller target lag prevents downstream dependencies

questions.explanation: A is correct because tighter freshness objectives typically drive more frequent refresh work. The other effects are not general guarantees of TARGET_LAG.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic table refresh cost/freshness considerations

questions.stem: Why might an engineer replace a manually orchestrated task chain with dynamic tables?

options.text: To let Snowflake manage refresh logic declaratively when SQL-based transformations are sufficient
Because dynamic tables can run Python handlers directly instead of SQL
Because dynamic tables are only for external stages
Because task graphs cannot have dependencies

questions.explanation: A is correct because dynamic tables are useful when the pipeline can be expressed declaratively in SQL. B, C, and D are incorrect.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables compared to streams and tasks

questions.stem: Which description best matches the relationship between dynamic tables and streams/tasks?

options.text: They are alternative patterns for some transformation workloads, each with different operational tradeoffs
Dynamic tables are only a user interface for creating tasks
Streams are required on every dynamic table
Tasks cannot coexist with dynamic tables in one account

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake documents them as different design patterns with distinct strengths. The other statements are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables compared to streams and tasks

questions.stem: What is the primary user-facing benefit of querying a dynamic table instead of recomputing a heavy transformation each time?

options.text: The transformed result is already materialized and maintained for reuse
It runs only in external compute
It forces all queries into the result cache forever

questions.explanation: A is correct because dynamic tables maintain reusable transformed results. The other answers misstate how they work.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Dynamic tables overview

questions.stem: What is Snowpark primarily for?

options.text: Building data engineering, transformation, and application logic in familiar languages while processing data in Snowflake

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpark lets developers use languages such as Python, Scala, or Java while pushing execution into Snowflake. It complements rather than replaces SQL.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark overview

questions.stem: Which statement best describes Snowpark DataFrame execution?

options.text: Transformations are compiled into operations that execute in Snowflake rather than on the client by default
Every transformation immediately downloads all rows to the laptop
It requires exporting data to pandas before any filter can run
It can only query external stages

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpark is designed to execute operations in Snowflake. B and C describe anti-patterns, and D is false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark DataFrames

questions.stem: When is to_pandas() generally the least desirable choice in a Snowpark pipeline?

options.text: When the dataset is large and the processing could stay in Snowflake
When debugging a tiny sample locally
When collecting a few metadata rows

questions.explanation: A is correct because bringing a large dataset to the client undermines scalable in-platform execution. The other cases may be reasonable for small outputs.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark pandas / DataFrame collection patterns

questions.stem: Why are Snowpark stored procedures useful in data engineering pipelines?

options.text: They package procedural logic close to the data and can be orchestrated from Snowflake
They eliminate the need for permissions
They are only for UI dashboards

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpark stored procedures encapsulate logic that runs in Snowflake and can participate in pipelines. They still need permissions, are not dashboard-only, and can interact with SQL.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark stored procedures

questions.stem: A developer wants to call external services from Snowpark code securely. Which Snowflake concept helps control that access?

options.text: External access integrations / related secure integration controls

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake uses integration-based controls for governed external connectivity. Clustering, result cache, and streams do not manage outbound service access.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — External network access / integrations for handlers

questions.stem: A transformation contains filters, joins, and aggregations that could all be expressed in Snowpark DataFrame code. What is the main reason to keep them in Snowpark instead of extracting data first?

options.text: It preserves pushdown and keeps computation close to the data
It turns every query into a materialized view

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpark’s value is in-platform execution and pushdown. The other options are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark DataFrame execution model

questions.stem: Which object do Snowpark developers commonly use as the session entry point in Python?
questions.explanation: A is correct because Session is the main Snowpark entry point for executing logic. The other objects are unrelated to Snowpark application code.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark Session API

questions.stem: A team needs reusable row-level business logic in SQL and Snowpark pipelines. Which choice best centralizes that logic?
questions.explanation: A is correct because a UDF packages reusable logic callable from queries or procedures. The other options serve administration or CDC purposes.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — User-defined functions

questions.stem: Which statement about Snowpark and SQL is most accurate?

options.text: Snowpark complements SQL; teams can combine both in one solution
Using Snowpark means SQL objects like views and tables can no longer be queried
Snowpark is only for data science and not data engineering

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpark and SQL are complementary. The other answers incorrectly frame Snowpark as a replacement or a limitation.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark overview

questions.stem: A Snowpark job needs package dependencies and repeatable deployment. What is the most important engineering concern?

options.text: Managing dependency versions and runtime environment consistently across environments
Disabling RBAC so the code can run everywhere
Dropping all staging tables after every run regardless of purpose

questions.explanation: A is correct because reproducible dependencies are central to stable Snowpark deployments. The other options are unsafe or unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark package and deployment guidance

questions.stem: What is one advantage of Snowpark Python over manually exporting data to a separate Python environment for every transformation?

options.text: It reduces data movement and keeps governance closer to the computation
It eliminates the need for warehouses forever

questions.explanation: A is correct because in-platform execution minimizes unnecessary movement and preserves Snowflake governance controls. The other choices are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark Python overview

questions.stem: Which output pattern is typically best when a Snowpark transformation result should feed downstream SQL analytics?

options.text: Persist the result to a table or view in Snowflake
Always print rows to the console only
Drop the result after each test regardless of requirements

questions.explanation: A is correct because downstream analytics work best when results are persisted in Snowflake objects. Console output and manual emailing are not durable pipeline outputs.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Snowpark write / save patterns

questions.stem: Which SQL statement is best for applying inserts, updates, and deletes from a staged change set into a target table in one declarative operation?
questions.explanation: A is correct because MERGE supports matched and not-matched logic for change application. TRUNCATE, REVOKE, and UNDROP are unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — MERGE

questions.stem: Why is MERGE often paired with streams in Snowflake CDC pipelines?

options.text: Streams provide changed rows and MERGE applies those changes to the target in one statement
MERGE is required to create a stream
Streams can only be queried inside MERGE and nowhere else
MERGE automatically shares data with external accounts

questions.explanation: A is correct because stream outputs naturally drive MERGE logic for upserts/deletes. The other statements are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Streams and MERGE patterns

questions.stem: A team loads raw events into a VARIANT column and later extracts fields into typed analytics columns. What is the main benefit of this two-step design?

options.text: It separates flexible ingestion from stricter downstream modeling
It eliminates the need for warehouses
It prevents any schema evolution forever

questions.explanation: A is correct because the pattern supports flexible arrival and deliberate downstream standardization. The other answers are false.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Semi-structured data transformations

questions.stem: Which function family is commonly used when exploring or extracting attributes from VARIANT data?
questions.explanation: A is correct because semi-structured querying often relies on path access and FLATTEN for arrays/objects. The other choices are unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — FLATTEN and querying VARIANT

questions.stem: When designing idempotent transformations, which property is most desirable?

options.text: Re-running the step produces the same correct target state without duplicating effects
The step can never be run twice
The step must truncate every table before processing

questions.explanation: A is correct because idempotent design lets retries or replays complete safely. B, C, and D are not reliable definitions of idempotency.
questions.reference: General data engineering best practice aligned to Snowflake pipeline design

questions.stem: Which SQL construct is most appropriate for producing a curated denormalized reporting table from several normalized source tables?

options.text: A SELECT with joins and aggregations, persisted to a target table or dynamic table

questions.explanation: A is correct because denormalized reporting layers are typically built from joins and aggregations persisted into curated objects. The other statements do not transform data.
questions.reference: Snowflake official certification overview — transform data for downstream use

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a clustering key on a large table?

options.text: To improve pruning for selective queries on relevant columns
To encrypt the table with a customer key

questions.explanation: A is correct because clustering improves micro-partition organization and pruning for query patterns that benefit from it. The other answers are unrelated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Clustering keys

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature is especially useful for accelerating highly selective point-lookups on large tables when pruning alone is not enough?
questions.explanation: A is correct because Search Optimization Service accelerates selective lookups. The other options are not query acceleration features.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Search Optimization Service

questions.stem: A query is slow because too little compute is available for the workload. Which change is the most direct first response?

options.text: Resize the warehouse appropriately for the workload

questions.explanation: A is correct because warehouse sizing directly affects available compute for execution. The other actions do not directly address compute shortfall.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Virtual warehouses and sizing

questions.stem: Why should engineers review query profiles when tuning SQL workloads?

options.text: They reveal execution bottlenecks such as scans, joins, spills, and skewed steps

questions.explanation: A is correct because query profiles expose where time and resources are spent during execution. The other answers misstate their purpose.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Query profile and query history

questions.stem: A workload has unpredictable concurrency spikes from many small analytic queries. Which warehouse feature is designed to help handle that pattern?
questions.explanation: A is correct because multi-cluster warehouses add compute clusters to absorb concurrency. The other features do not manage concurrent query pressure.
questions.reference: Snowflake Docs — Multi-cluster warehouses
